name: FTC Trade Regulation Rule on Unfair or Deceptive Fees (Junk Fees Rule)
summary: Federal rule requiring clear disclosure of total price including all mandatory fees for live-event tickets and short-term lodging.

evidence.claim: FTC finalized the Junk Fees Rule on December 17, 2024, effective May 12, 2025.
The final Rule is significantly narrower than the original proposal; it applies only to live-event tickets and short-term lodging.
All non-optional fees must be included in the Total Price. Government taxes, shipping, and optional ancillary services do NOT need to be included in the Total Price.
FTC estimates the rule will save consumers ~53 million hours per year, equivalent to ~$11 billion over the next decade.

body.officialName: Trade Regulation Rule on Unfair or Deceptive Fees
body.scopeSummary: Prohibits (1) hidden fees — failing to clearly and conspicuously disclose mandatory fees in the Total Price — and (2) misleading fees — misrepresenting the nature or amount of fees. Scope is LIMITED to live-event tickets and short-term lodging (E2).
body.userRightsPlainLanguage: When you buy a live-event ticket or book short-term lodging in the US, the price you see up front must include all mandatory fees. Government taxes, shipping, and OPTIONAL add-ons can be shown separately. If a ticket site or hotel surprises you with a mandatory 'service fee' or 'resort fee' only at checkout, they are violating this rule.

enforcementSignals.description: File a report with the FTC for deceptive pricing.
State Attorneys General also enforce deceptive-pricing laws.
